steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the passage most strongly suggests about specimens from natural history collections. The key tension: the approach of using hDNA from these specimens "offers unique benefits" yet "remains relatively underutilized because archival specimens are sometimes stored in ways that compromise DNA quality" — a problem "not easily remediable." The correct answer must reflect both the value of specimens and the barrier to using them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'While they may contain valuable genomic data, not all of them can yield usable hDNA.': directly captures the dual reality: value (unique benefits, usable data) plus limitation (poor storage compromises DNA). ✓
  • Choice B — 'They are primarily used as sources of genomic data by scientists studying organisms that have undergone major evolutionary change.': contradicts the passage, which says the approach remains "relatively underutilized" — it is not a primary method. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Because of their often-deteriorated condition, they tend to yield genomic data that are time consuming to extract and interpret.': the passage mentions compromised DNA quality, not time-consuming extraction — this is unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — 'While they were used in a study that made an important scientific discovery.': incomplete and does not capture the suggestion about specimens generally; it only describes the one cited study. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
